writing_task: Hey, I could use some help with our year-end major donor appeal — specifically for the subset of donors who gave at the $5,000+ level last year. We're highlighting our workforce development program, and I have a really compelling story about a guy named James who completed our welding certification track and just got hired at a manufacturing plant at nearly double his previous income. I want this letter to feel like a strategic debrief, not a thank-you card — these are sophisticated donors who want to know their investment moved the needle, and I need the ask to ladder up to a specific expansion goal we have for next year. Can you help me draft something around 400-450 words?

drafts.rubric:
Dear [Donor Name],

James walked into our welding certification track carrying a forklift operator's wages and a specific calculation: he needed skills that would hold, not a credential that would stall. Six months later, he signed an offer letter from Meridian Manufacturing — $28 an hour, nearly double what he'd been making. He made that call. He showed up every morning and put in the hours.

Your investment last year helped build the infrastructure James walked into — the equipment, the instructors, the employer partnerships. And James wasn't alone. Forty-one people moved through the same track in 2023. Thirty-four secured jobs in their target field within ninety days of completion. The average wage gain across that cohort was 74%.

Here is where the calculus gets straightforward. That program is currently constrained to a single cohort per quarter. The waitlist is real — forty-plus people at any given time, each one running their own version of James's math. We've identified a facility partner and have instructor capacity to run a second cohort simultaneously. The gap is $175,000, and with it we project an additional 80 placements in 2024.

Your prior decision compounded. It funded a program that produced measurable wage mobility at scale. The logical next move is to double the throughput — same model, same employer network, twice the reach.

James just finished his third week on the floor at Meridian. Forty people on that waitlist are still doing the math. A renewed commitment at or above your 2023 level moves this expansion from projected to funded.

I'd welcome a conversation before December 31st if you'd like to walk through the numbers in more detail.

With respect,

[Signature]
drafts.generic:
Dear [Donor Name],

Last year, you made a strategic bet on economic mobility — and the returns are coming in.

James enrolled in our welding certification track unemployed and uncertain. Fourteen weeks later, he accepted an offer from a regional manufacturing plant at nearly double his previous income. His story is not an outlier. It is what your $5,000 investment was engineered to produce.

Our workforce development program placed 47 participants in living-wage positions this year, with an average income increase of 74%. Employers are calling us before positions are posted.

That demand is exactly why we are expanding. In 2025, we are scaling the welding track to serve 30 additional participants, requiring $180,000 in committed funding before March. Your renewed gift at the $5,000 level — or above — directly funds one participant's full certification, tools, and job placement support.

We are not asking you to sustain something. We are asking you to scale something that works.

I would welcome a brief conversation before year-end. In the meantime, your gift can be made at [link] or by returning the enclosed card.

With appreciation and momentum,

[Signature]
drafts.preference:
Dear [First Name],

Last year, you made a deliberate investment in workforce development — and I want to tell you exactly what that looked like on the ground.

James came to us working a physically demanding job that paid just over $14 an hour. He enrolled in our welding certification track, showed up every morning for 12 weeks, and passed his credentialing exam on the first attempt. Last spring, he accepted an offer from a regional manufacturing plant at $26 an hour. That's not a rounding error. That's a different life.

Your gift helped make that possible — the equipment, the instructors, the wraparound support that kept James enrolled when things got hard. We ran this program for 47 participants last year, and James's outcome was not the exception. It was close to the rule.

Here's where we are heading next: we've identified a clear opportunity to expand the welding track and add two new certification pathways — electrical and HVAC — in response to direct demand from regional employers who are actively struggling to fill skilled trades roles. We've mapped out the budget, we've secured the partnerships, and we're ready to move. What we need is the funding to launch.

To reach our expansion goal and serve 80 additional participants in the coming year, we need to raise $250,000 by December 31st. We're asking donors at your level to consider a gift of $10,000 — an amount that, based on our per-participant costs, directly funds the full program experience for roughly three people, start to finish.

This isn't a moment where we're testing whether something might work. We know it works. We've watched it work. We're asking you to help us do more of it, at a scale that matches the need.

If you'd like to talk through the expansion plan in more detail — the employer pipeline, the outcome metrics, the timeline — I'd genuinely welcome that conversation. And if you're ready to make your gift now, you can do so at [website] or by returning the enclosed reply card.

Thank you for being the kind of donor who asks hard questions and expects real answers. That's exactly the kind of partnership we need.

With gratitude and respect,

[Signature]
[Title]
[Organization]

user_reason: Draft B is the strongest by a significant margin. James is the subject of his own story — 'he made that call,' 'he showed up,' 'he signed' — which is exactly how I want beneficiaries framed. The donor is positioned as someone whose 'investment compounded,' which hits the strategic-collaborator framing I need for this audience. Story comes first, then numbers as evidence of scale. The ask connects directly back to the waitlist and the specific gap. The only issue is it got cut off, but the bones are right. Draft A is solid — warm, professional, specific ask tied to the story — but it slips into 'We ran this program' and 'Your gift helped make that possible' which edges toward benefactor framing rather than strategic partner framing. The per-participant cost breakdown is smart though. Draft C is the weakest despite feeling the most polished. 'James enrolled... unemployed and uncertain' is passive and a little pitying. 'Engineered to produce' is corporate jargon that feels cold. Biggest problem: 'We are not asking you to sustain something. We are asking you to scale something' — that 'We' as actor framing makes the nonprofit the hero, which is exactly what I want to avoid. The brevity reads as efficient but sacrifices the story depth that makes the number meaningful.

criteria_analysis.name: Community-Centered Voice
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with James as the grammatical subject and keeps him as protagonist throughout — 'James walked,' 'He made that call,' 'He showed up' — with the organization almost invisible as a subject. The generic draft centers the organization repeatedly ('Our workforce development program placed,' 'We are scaling,' 'We are not asking') and frames James as someone who 'enrolled' rather than acted with full agency. The original preference draft is better than generic but still has substantial org-as-subject framing ('We ran this program,' 'we've identified,' 'We know it works').

criteria_analysis.name: Story-First Evidence Integration
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft establishes James's full story — his calculation, his action, his outcome — before any number appears, and the data (41 people, 34 placements, 74% wage gain) lands as confirmation of what James's story already demonstrated. The original preference draft also leads with story before data but introduces numbers somewhat earlier and with slightly less earned context. The generic draft introduces James briefly then pivots quickly to aggregate statistics ('placed 47 participants') that feel more like a pitch than confirmation.

criteria_analysis.name: Protagonist Continuity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is exceptional here — James reappears by name in the closing ('James just finished his third week on the floor at Meridian'), providing a specific, concrete closing image that echoes his opening and completes his arc. The generic draft drops James entirely after the second paragraph; he never returns. The original preference draft also drops James after the opening narrative and closes with abstract language about partnership and gratitude rather than a specific image of a named person.

criteria_analysis.name: Earned and Specific Ask
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft's ask emerges organically from the waitlist narrative — the $175,000 gap and 80 projected placements feel like the logical next chapter after James's story, and it connects directly back to him ('Forty people on that waitlist are still doing the math'). The original preference draft has a specific ask ($10,000, 80 participants, December 31) that's well-bounded but arrives more as a fundraising block than an organic extension of James's story. The generic draft has specifics ($5,000, 30 participants) but the ask feels more transactional and disconnected from the narrative.

criteria_analysis.name: Active, Concrete Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is loaded with precise motion verbs — 'walked into,' 'signed an offer letter,' 'showed up every morning,' 'running their own version of James's math' — and every emotional peak has a specific, visual image. The original preference draft also uses strong verbs ('showed up every morning,' 'passed his credentialing exam') but has more abstract passages in the middle and close. The generic draft relies more on states ('unemployed and uncertain,' 'is not an outlier') and generic framing ('strategic bet on economic mobility').

criteria_analysis.name: Strategic Donor Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels with 'Your prior decision compounded,' 'the calculus gets straightforward,' 'The logical next move,' and 'A renewed commitment' — all strategic/financial vocabulary that positions the donor as an effective investor. It frames the ask as continuation ('same model, same employer network, twice the reach'). The generic draft uses 'strategic bet' and 'engineered to produce' but also slips into charitable framing ('With appreciation and momentum'). The original preference draft acknowledges the donor's sophistication ('asks hard questions') but frames giving more as generosity ('With gratitude and respect') than as strategic decision-making.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is a near-perfect execution of every criterion this user values — James as protagonist with full agency throughout, story earning the data, protagonist continuity through the close, an organically emerging ask, precise active verbs, and strategic donor framing that mirrors the user's own language preferences (especially 'calculus,' 'compounded,' 'logical next move'). The original preference draft is solid fundraising writing but loses protagonist continuity and defaults to org-centered framing in its middle and close. The generic draft is competent but violates nearly every priority this user holds — it drops James, centers the organization, and frames the donor as generous rather than strategic.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both drafts communicate their message with precision. Draft A provides more granular detail (specific cohort sizes, waitlist numbers, facility partner), while Draft B is more streamlined; both achieve clarity effectively.

Draft A builds a tightly logical progression — individual story, aggregate data, constraint, solution, ask — with each paragraph flowing naturally from the last. Draft B follows a similar arc but the transition from results to expansion feels slightly more abrupt.
Both drafts are mechanically clean with no grammatical errors, typos, or punctuation issues.

Both are well-organized with clear paragraph functions: hook, impact evidence, the ask, and closing. Draft B is more concise; Draft A is more detailed — both structures serve their respective approaches well.

Draft A's opening is more textured and immersive ('carrying a forklift operator's wages and a specific calculation'), and the closing callback to James and the waitlist creates compelling tension. Draft B is punchy and effective but slightly more formulaic in its fundraising cadence.

Draft A maintains a distinctive, confident, respectful voice throughout — treating the donor as an analytical peer without lapsing into flattery. Draft B's tone is strong but edges toward conventional fundraising language ('strategic bet,' 'with appreciation and momentum'), which slightly diminishes its distinctiveness.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece overall: its voice is more distinctive and assured, its narrative arc is more carefully constructed with a satisfying callback, and it treats the donor with a level of intellectual respect that elevates it above conventional fundraising appeals. Draft B is polished and effective but reads as a well-executed template rather than a standout letter.

- **Protagonist Continuity**: The rubric-guided draft scored highest here and the user's comments confirm protagonist continuity is a core differentiator — the original preference draft and generic draft both dropped James, which the user did not flag as a concern but the scores confirm as a weakness in those drafts. Kept unchanged.
- **Earned and Specific Ask**: The rubric-guided draft scored highest and the user specifically praised 'the ask connects directly back to the waitlist and the specific gap' — the criterion is capturing the right behavior. Kept unchanged.
- **Active, Concrete Language**: The rubric-guided draft scored highest and the user's critique of the generic draft ('unemployed and uncertain is passive and a little pitying') confirms this criterion is working. Kept unchanged.
- **Strategic Donor Framing**: The user ranked the rubric-guided draft first and praised 'investment compounded' and the strategic-collaborator framing, but their critique of the original preference draft reveals a sharper edge to this criterion than the current description captures — specifically, the user flagged 'Your gift helped make that possible' as slipping toward benefactor framing, and 'We ran this program' as making the nonprofit the hero. Sharpened the description and added a dimension to explicitly flag org-as-hero language as a failure mode of this criterion, since both the original preference and generic drafts lost points here for reasons the current wording doesn't quite name.

suggested_rubric.name: Community-Centered Voice
suggested_rubric.description: Community members — not the organization — are the grammatical subjects and protagonists of the piece. The organization is present in the implied infrastructure but does not narrate its own work. Beneficiaries are shown choosing, deciding, and acting, never positioned as passive recipients.

dimensions.label: Community members (individuals or families) are the grammatical subjects of most key sentences
Beneficiaries are shown exercising agency — choosing, deciding, walking toward — rather than receiving services
The organization rarely appears as a grammatical subject; its role is implied through infrastructure, not narrated as achievement
The piece avoids 'look what we accomplished' framing in favor of 'here is what is happening in these communities'

suggested_rubric.name: Story-First Evidence Integration
suggested_rubric.description: Human narrative always precedes data. Numbers arrive as confirmation of what the reader already feels, not as the opening argument. When data reappears later in the piece, it callbacks to the narrative that earned it, anchoring the evidence to the emotional through-line.

dimensions.label: A human image or story is established before any impact number is introduced
Data points land as confirmation of the story's significance rather than as a standalone pitch for impact
When numbers reappear in later sections, they are tied back to the narrative context that originally earned them
Scale emerges from individual stories outward (specific person → broader cohort) rather than from aggregate statistics downward

suggested_rubric.name: Protagonist Continuity
suggested_rubric.description: Named individuals introduced early in the piece remain present and active throughout, especially in the closing. The protagonist's arc must arrive somewhere — callbacks, echoes, and parallel structure connect opening images to closing images so the emotional through-line doesn't fade at the moment it matters most.

dimensions.label: Named individuals from the opening reappear by name in or near the closing section
The protagonist's story has a discernible arc — it arrives somewhere rather than simply being introduced and dropped
Closing images deliberately echo or mirror opening images (parallel actions, callbacks, repeated motifs)
The final emotional image is a specific named person doing something, not a generalized outcome

suggested_rubric.name: Earned and Specific Ask
suggested_rubric.description: The call to action grows organically from the narrative rather than being appended as a fundraising block. It is specific and bounded — a named dollar amount, a defined number of units, a clear deadline — and connects directly to the protagonist's story so the donor sees the gift as the next chapter, not a separate transaction.

dimensions.label: The ask emerges from the narrative as a logical next step rather than arriving as a tacked-on appeal
The ask includes a specific dollar amount, a defined number of units or people, and/or a clear timeframe
The ask connects directly back to the named protagonist's story or situation
The close creates forward momentum and invitation rather than gratitude and closure

suggested_rubric.name: Active, Concrete Language
suggested_rubric.description: Verbs show motion and action rather than states and readiness. At key emotional moments — especially openings and closings — language is concrete and imagistic rather than abstract. The reader should be able to picture someone doing something, not just understand that something is the case.

dimensions.label: Key sentences use verbs of action and motion (walked, walking, stepped) rather than states (is ready, are able, are at the threshold)
Emotional high points use specific, visual imagery rather than abstract or generalized language
Descriptions reference particular people, places, and moments rather than categories or types
Verb choices are deliberate and precise — each verb earns its place and carries the intended energy (active, volitional, forward)

suggested_rubric.name: Strategic Donor Framing
suggested_rubric.description: Donors are positioned as effective strategic investors whose past decisions produced specific measurable outcomes, not as generous benefactors being thanked. Language borrows from strategy and finance — 'compounds,' 'logical next move,' 'calculus' — to activate the donor's identity as someone who makes smart calls, not someone who gives charitably. The organization must not appear as the hero or actor of the outcomes; framing the nonprofit as the agent ('we ran,' 'we built,' 'your gift helped us') collapses the donor's strategic identity into a supporting role and must be avoided.

dimensions.label: Past giving is credited with producing a specific, named outcome rather than appreciated abstractly
Uses strategic or financial vocabulary (e.g., 'compounds,' 'calculus,' 'logical next move') to speak to the donor's investor identity
Donor is made to feel effective and smart, not generous and appreciated
The ask is framed as a continuation of an existing strategy, not a new request for help

The organization is not positioned as the actor driving outcomes — phrases like 'your gift helped us' or 'we ran this program' are avoided because they make the nonprofit the hero and reduce the donor to a funder

writing_task: Hey! I'm working on our year-end giving appeal and I need help drafting something for a specific segment of our donor list — people who gave for the first time last year but haven't given again since. The program angle is our workforce development initiative: we've got a compelling story about a man named Darnell who completed our welding certification program, negotiated his starting wage, and is now mentoring two newer participants. I want this to feel like a re-engagement letter that reminds lapsed donors why their first investment mattered and makes a clear case for why now is the right moment to give again — without it coming across as guilt-trippy or desperate.

drafts.rubric:
Dear [First Name],

Last year, you made a calculated bet. You put your dollars behind a man named Darnell — and Darnell delivered.

He walked into a welding certification program carrying nothing but intention. He completed every module, then sat across a table from a prospective employer and negotiated his starting wage. Not accepted it. Negotiated it. Today he shows up early on Tuesdays to mentor two newer participants who are working through the same curriculum he finished.

Your first investment produced that. The question the data keeps raising is straightforward: what does a second one compound into?

Right now, eleven people are sitting where Darnell sat twelve months ago. A gift of $1,500 covers one participant's full certification costs — materials, assessments, the works — and gets them to the table where Darnell learned to hold his ground. The deadline to fund this cohort is December 31.

Darnell is already in the room on Tuesdays, passing something forward. The logical next move is to make sure the seat across from him stays filled.

[Giving link]
drafts.generic:
Dear Friend,

A year ago, you made a choice to invest in someone's future. That choice mattered more than you may know.

Meet Darnell. When he joined our workforce development program, he was looking for a way forward. He completed our welding certification, negotiated his own starting wage, and today he's mentoring two newer participants through the same journey you helped make possible.

Stories like Darnell's begin with donors like you. We're hoping you'll renew that commitment this year — not because we're asking you to, but because you've already shown you believe this work is worth it.

Will you give again today?
drafts.preference:
Dear [First Name],

A little over a year ago, you made your first gift to [Organization Name]. We wanted to take a moment to tell you what happened next.

Darnell came to our welding certification program without a clear path forward. He put in the work, earned his certification, and then did something that surprised even his instructors — he negotiated his starting wage. He knew what he was worth. Today he's mentoring two newer participants through the same program.

That's your first gift, still working.

As the year ends, we'd love to have you back. A gift of any size today helps us reach the next Darnell. Will you give again at [donation link]?

With gratitude,
[Signature]

user_reason: Draft A is the clear winner. It centers Darnell's agency throughout — 'he negotiated,' 'he shows up,' 'he's passing something forward' — and frames the donor as a strategic investor making a calculated bet, not a generous benefactor. The ask is specific and tied directly to the story: $1,500, one participant's full certification, December 31 deadline, eleven people currently in the cohort. The 'compound' framing makes the donor feel smart. Draft B is serviceable but weaker — it opens with 'We wanted to take a moment' which starts the nonprofit-as-narrator problem, and 'the next Darnell' is a vague generic ask rather than something anchored to the story. 'With gratitude' also slides toward benefactor framing. Draft C has real problems for me: 'Dear Friend' is impersonal and lazy, 'you helped make possible' subtly shifts Darnell's achievement toward donor credit, and 'donors like you' is exactly the virtuous-benefactor framing I want to avoid. 'We're hoping you'll renew' reads as slightly guilt-adjacent, and the ask is completely unspecific — no number, no outcome tied to the story.

criteria_analysis.name: Community-Centered Voice
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft makes Darnell the grammatical subject throughout — he walked, completed, sat, negotiated, shows up, mentors — and the organization never appears as a subject. The generic draft uses 'our workforce development program,' 'our welding certification,' and 'we're hoping,' centering the org multiple times. The original preference draft is better ('He put in the work, earned his certification') but still uses 'our welding certification program' and 'we wanted to take a moment,' giving the org a narrator role.

criteria_analysis.name: Strategic Donor Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails the strategic investor register: 'calculated bet,' 'Your first investment produced that,' 'what does a second one compound into,' 'logical next move' — this is exactly the language the user praised in the conversation history. The generic draft falls into the generous-benefactor frame ('you made a choice to invest,' 'we're hoping you'll renew that commitment,' 'you believe this work is worth it') — appreciative rather than strategic. The original preference draft connects past gift to outcome ('That's your first gift, still working') which is solid but lacks the strategic/financial vocabulary and investor-identity activation.

criteria_analysis.name: Earned and Specific Ask
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft includes a specific dollar amount ($1,500), a defined unit (one participant's full certification), a deadline (December 31), and the ask emerges directly from the narrative about Darnell's cohort successors. The generic draft has no dollar amount, no deadline, no specificity — 'Will you give again today?' is as generic as it gets. The original preference draft says 'a gift of any size' which is intentionally unspecific, though it does connect back to 'the next Darnell.'

criteria_analysis.name: Story-First Evidence Integration
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft establishes Darnell's full story before introducing the number eleven and the $1,500 figure, and scales using protagonist-sized units ('eleven people are sitting where Darnell sat'). The generic draft introduces Darnell's story but never uses data at all — there are no numbers to integrate. The original preference draft also leads with story before any ask, and 'the next Darnell' uses the protagonist as a unit of measurement, but doesn't offer the same layered data integration.

criteria_analysis.name: Active, Concrete Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is packed with motion verbs and concrete imagery: 'walked into,' 'sat across a table,' 'negotiated his starting wage,' 'shows up early on Tuesdays,' 'the seat across from him stays filled.' The generic draft relies on state verbs and abstractions: 'was looking for a way forward,' 'stories like Darnell's begin with,' 'you've already shown you believe.' The original preference draft has strong moments ('negotiated his starting wage,' 'He knew what he was worth') but also softer, less visual language ('came to our program without a clear path forward').

criteria_analysis.name: Protagonist Continuity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft keeps Darnell present from opening to closing — he's named in the final paragraph ('Darnell is already in the room on Tuesdays') and the ask connects directly to his mentoring scene ('make sure the seat across from him stays filled'). This is exactly the non-negotiable the coaching notes flag. The generic draft drops Darnell entirely after the second paragraph; the close is abstract ('Will you give again today?'). The original preference draft uses 'the next Darnell' at the close, which echoes him but doesn't keep the actual named person active and present in the ask paragraph.

The original preference draft has strong moments ('negotiated his starting wage,' 'He knew what he was worth') but also softer, less visual language ('came to our program without a clear path forward').

The rubric-guided draft keeps Darnell present from opening to closing — he's named in the final paragraph ('Darnell is already in the room on Tuesdays') and the ask connects directly to his mentoring scene ('make sure the seat across from him stays filled'). This is exactly the non-negotiable the coaching notes flag. The generic draft drops Darnell entirely after the second paragraph; the close is abstract ('Will you give again today?'). The original preference draft uses 'the next Darnell' at the close, which echoes him but doesn't keep the actual named person active and present in the ask paragraph.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across every criterion — it delivers the exact strategic-investor register the user praised in conversation, keeps Darnell active and named through the closing (the user's most frequently revised element), and builds an earned, specific ask that emerges organically from the narrative. The original preference draft is competent and warm but lacks the strategic vocabulary, specificity in the ask, and protagonist presence at the close that this user has shown are non-negotiable. The generic draft falls short on nearly every dimension, centering the organization, using passive/abstract language, and offering no specificity in the ask.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is exceptionally clear, with a specific dollar amount, deadline, and concrete details about what the gift covers. Draft B is clear but vague — it lacks specifics about what the renewed gift would fund.

Draft A builds a tight logical arc: past investment → result → compounding question → specific next step → callback to Darnell mentoring. Draft B follows a logical sequence but the transition from story to ask feels more conventional and less tightly threaded.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors, typos, and mechanical issues. Both demonstrate polished, professional writing.

Draft A is masterfully structured — each paragraph serves a distinct function (hook, story, data-driven pivot, specific ask, emotional close). Draft B is well-organized but more formulaic in its standard nonprofit appeal structure.

Draft A is deeply compelling — 'Not accepted it. Negotiated it' is a standout moment, and the investment/compounding metaphor sustains reader interest. Draft B reads like a competent but generic fundraising letter that doesn't distinguish itself.

Draft A strikes a distinctive tone — confident, respectful of the reader's intelligence, and free of sentimentality while still being emotionally resonant. Draft B's tone is warm and appropriate but relies on familiar nonprofit phrasing ('Stories like Darnell's begin with donors like you') that feels less fresh.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is superior across nearly every dimension — it's more specific, more engaging, more structurally sophisticated, and more distinctive in voice. It treats the reader as a strategic partner rather than defaulting to generic donor-appreciation language, and it closes with a memorable image that ties the entire letter together.

writing_task: Hey! I'm putting together a mid-year impact report for our major donors — people who gave $10,000 or more last year — and I need help drafting the centerpiece story for it. We run a workforce development program for formerly incarcerated adults, and I have notes on a specific participant named Darnell who completed our welding certification track, got hired at a manufacturing company, and just got promoted to shift supervisor six months in. I want the piece to feel substantive and credible for this more sophisticated donor audience, not like a feel-good fluff piece — these are people who think in terms of ROI and systems change, but I still want Darnell's story to land emotionally. Can you help me draft something around 400 words?

drafts.rubric:
Darnell walked into his first welding class carrying a notebook and a record that most employers never look past. He had decided, while still incarcerated, that he was going to learn a trade — not because someone told him to, but because he had run the numbers himself and decided the risk of staying still was greater than the risk of starting over.

Fourteen months later, he is a shift supervisor at a mid-size manufacturing company, responsible for a team of nine, managing production schedules, and mentoring the newer hires who remind him of where he started. His employer promoted him six months in — faster than most of their general hires. That wasn't charity. That was performance.

Darnell's outcome is not an anomaly. Across this year's cohort, participants who completed the certification track are employed at a rate of 74 percent at six months — in an economy where formerly incarcerated adults face unemployment rates four to five times the national average. Every one of those numbers is a person who made the same bet Darnell made. The number doesn't create the story. It counts it.

Your investment last year helped build the infrastructure that made Darnell's cohort possible — the certified instruction, the employer partnerships, the credentialing pipeline. That calculus is already proven. The question now is how many more cohorts it compounds across.

The next cohort begins in September. Funding fifteen additional participants through full certification — at $4,200 per seat — closes this year's cohort to capacity and carries the program through the fiscal year. That's $63,000 to move fifteen more people from where Darnell started to where Darnell stands now: at the front of a shop floor, running a shift, with his name on a schedule he built himself.
drafts.generic:
When Darnell walked into our welding certification track eighteen months ago, he carried what most of our participants carry: a skills gap, a record, and a labor market that had already decided what those two things meant about his future. What he did not carry was a reason to believe that calculus would change.

Six months after completing the program, he was hired at Meridian Manufacturing. Six months after that, he was promoted to shift supervisor — managing a team, owning outcomes, and earning a wage that puts him firmly in the middle-income bracket for our region.

That trajectory is not accidental. It reflects a program design built around what employers actually need: industry-recognized credentials, soft-skills coaching that mirrors real workplace culture, and wraparound case management that addresses the practical barriers — transportation, housing instability, legal fees — that sink reentry employment before it starts. Darnell's success is the result of that system working as intended.

It also points toward something larger. Research consistently shows that stable employment is the single strongest predictor of reduced recidivism. Every participant who moves from incarceration to sustained, quality employment represents a measurable reduction in downstream costs to the criminal justice system, public benefits infrastructure, and the communities most burdened by cyclical poverty. The ROI, when modeled across a cohort, is not incremental — it compounds.

What the data cannot fully capture is what Darnell said when we asked him to reflect on the past year. He said he wanted his kids to see him differently. Not as a cautionary story, but as evidence that the story can change.

That is precisely the kind of outcome your investment makes possible — not a moment of relief, but a structural shift in what one life, and the lives around it, can look like going forward. Darnell is now a reference point for newer participants in the program. He comes back. He talks to the cohorts coming up behind him. He has become, without anyone asking him to, a proof point and a mentor.

That is systems change at its most concrete: a person who was locked out of the labor market now holding a door open for others. We are grateful for the partnership that makes it possible.
drafts.preference:
Six months ago, Darnell walked into a manufacturing facility in East Baltimore as a new hire. Last week, he walked in as shift supervisor.

That kind of trajectory doesn't happen by accident. Darnell completed our welding certification track after spending four years incarcerated — a period he describes with characteristic directness as "time I can't get back, but also time that made me serious." When he finished the program, he had a credential, a set of references who actually knew his work, and a connection to an employer who had already hired three of our graduates.

The promotion came after six months. His manager told our team that Darnell had a quality that's difficult to train: he understood what it felt like to need a second chance, so he gave his team members that same patience when they needed it. That's not a soft observation. That's a management philosophy, and it came directly from what he lived through.

This is what workforce development looks like when it's working — not just job placement, but genuine economic mobility for people the labor market has historically written off. The average cost of incarceration in Maryland runs over $40,000 per person per year. Our cost per successful job placement is a fraction of that. But beyond the math, Darnell is now paying taxes, mentoring newer graduates informally, and building the kind of stability that extends to his family and his neighborhood.

We're on track to place 47 participants this fiscal year, up from 31 last year. That growth is directly tied to the relationships we've built with regional employers — relationships your support helped us develop and sustain.

Here's what we're asking: if you've been thinking about renewing or increasing your gift before the year-end cycle, doing so now would give us the runway to launch a second welding cohort this fall. We have a waitlist. We have employer partners ready to receive graduates. What we need is the funding to move forward.

Darnell's story isn't the exception we hope for. It's the model we're working to replicate, systematically, with every person who comes through our doors. Your investment is what makes that replication possible.

user_reason: C is the clear winner. Darnell's agency is centered from the first sentence — he decided, he ran the numbers, he made the bet. The framing never positions the program as his savior. The story comes first, then the number as evidence of scale, exactly how I think about impact writing. 'That wasn't charity. That was performance.' is the kind of line that makes a sophisticated donor sit up straight. The donor address as strategic investor is implicit but present. And the ask is clearly building toward something specific — per-seat cost, next cohort, September — even though it's cut off. B is decent and has a strong opening with the before/after structure, and I genuinely like the manager's quote about Darnell's management philosophy — that's a real insight. But 'our welding certification track' and 'our team' creep in, and the framing occasionally slides toward what the program did for Darnell rather than what Darnell built. The cost-per-placement comparison is smart for this audience. A loses me early. 'What he did not carry was a reason to believe' is the kind of sentence that sounds literary but actually positions Darnell as someone without hope until intervention — that's exactly the framing I want to avoid. 'A program design built around what employers actually need' makes the program the hero. The ROI language is fine for this audience but it's doing all the heavy lifting because Darnell barely exists as a person in this draft.

criteria_analysis.name: Community-Centered Voice
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft keeps Darnell as the grammatical subject throughout and the organization is virtually invisible — infrastructure is implied but never claimed. The generic draft repeatedly centers the organization ('our participants,' 'our team,' 'a program design built around what employers actually need,' 'We are grateful') and explicitly claims credit for systems. The original preference draft is better but still uses 'our' framing frequently ('our welding certification track,' 'our cost per successful job placement,' 'our team') and has an organizational-voice paragraph about placement numbers.

criteria_analysis.name: Story-First Evidence Integration
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft masterfully builds Darnell's full arc before any number appears, and when 74% lands, it arrives as confirmation of a story already felt — 'Every one of those numbers is a person who made the same bet Darnell made' ties data back to narrative perfectly. The original preference draft establishes story before data but introduces the $40,000 incarceration cost comparison somewhat abruptly as an external data point. The generic draft integrates data reasonably but leans into programmatic description and research citations mid-piece, which reads more like a pitch than confirmation.

criteria_analysis.name: Protagonist Continuity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft closes with Darnell by name 'at the front of a shop floor, running a shift, with his name on a schedule he built himself' — a concrete, specific image of the named protagonist in motion at the exact point of the ask. The generic draft brings Darnell back via a quote and as a mentor, but the closing dissolves into abstracted gratitude ('We are grateful for the partnership') with no protagonist image at the peak. The original preference draft ends on 'Your investment is what makes that replication possible' — Darnell is mentioned as a model but the final image is abstract and organizational, not Darnell in motion.

criteria_analysis.name: Earned and Specific Ask
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft has a precise ask ($4,200 per seat, 15 participants, $63,000, September cohort, fiscal year) that emerges organically from the narrative and connects directly back to Darnell's story in protagonist-sized units. The original preference draft has a clear ask (second welding cohort, year-end timing) but lacks a specific dollar amount and is slightly more organizational in framing. The generic draft has no ask at all — it closes with gratitude, which is exactly what this user's rubric warns against.

criteria_analysis.name: Active, Concrete Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is rich in motion verbs and concrete imagery: 'walked into,' 'ran the numbers himself,' 'managing production schedules,' 'at the front of a shop floor, running a shift, with his name on a schedule he built himself.' The original preference draft also has strong concrete moments ('walked into a manufacturing facility,' 'he understood what it felt like to need a second chance') but slips into abstraction in places ('genuine economic mobility'). The generic draft uses adequate language but frequently defaults to abstract or state-based constructions ('reflects a program design,' 'represents a measurable reduction,' 'is systems change at its most concrete').

criteria_analysis.name: Strategic Donor Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft uses 'calculus,' 'compounds,' 'infrastructure,' 'bet,' credits the donor's past investment with producing a specific named outcome, and frames the next gift as compounding an existing strategy — exactly the investor-identity language this user prefers. The original preference draft acknowledges past support ('relationships your support helped us develop') but frames the ask more as a traditional request ('Here's what we're asking') and uses some grateful/organizational framing. The generic draft closes with 'We are grateful for the partnership' — the exact register this user rejects — and frames the donor as a benefactor rather than a strategist.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across every criterion this user cares about: Darnell is the subject and agent throughout, the organization is invisible, data confirms story, the protagonist is present and concrete at the ask, the ask is specific and earned, and the donor is treated as a strategic investor. The original preference draft is solid but slips into organizational voice and lacks the protagonist-at-the-close discipline and strategic donor framing precision. The generic draft centers the organization, has no ask, and closes with gratitude — violating this user's core non-negotiables.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is exceptionally clear, with every sentence serving a precise purpose and the ask stated in concrete, unmistakable terms. Draft B is also clear but occasionally layers abstractions ('structural shift in what one life... can look like going forward') that slightly dilute directness.
Both drafts move logically from individual story to programmatic evidence to the funding ask/partnership appeal. Each paragraph in both pieces builds naturally on the last with strong internal logic.
Both drafts are mechanically flawless with no grammatical errors, typos, or punctuation issues.

Draft A has a tightly controlled arc — story, data, previous investment, specific ask — with nothing extraneous. Draft B is well-organized but slightly sprawling, covering program design, recidivism research, an emotional quote, mentorship, and a gratitude close, which diffuses the structural focus.

Draft A engages through lean, muscular prose and a striking closing image. Draft B engages through emotional depth and the powerful direct quote from Darnell about his kids. Both are compelling in different but equally effective ways.

Draft A maintains a remarkably consistent voice — confident, respectful, unsentimental — that treats the reader as a peer. Draft B is strong but oscillates between analytical nonprofit language ('wraparound case management,' 'ROI when modeled across a cohort') and warmer narrative moments, creating slight tonal unevenness.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece overall: it achieves the same emotional and intellectual impact as Draft B but in fewer words, with a more disciplined structure, a more consistent voice, and a sharper call to action. Draft B is excellent writing that offers richer programmatic detail and a moving quote, but its broader scope slightly dilutes its persuasive force.

suggested_rubric.name: Community-Centered Voice
suggested_rubric.description: The people served — not the organization — are the grammatical subjects and protagonists of the piece. The organization is present in implied infrastructure but does not narrate its own work or claim credit for capacity. Individuals are shown choosing, deciding, and acting, never positioned as passive recipients of services.

dimensions.label: Individuals served (named people, families, cohorts) are the grammatical subjects of most key sentences
People served are shown exercising agency — choosing, deciding, walking toward, betting on themselves, negotiating — rather than receiving interventions
The organization rarely appears as a grammatical subject; infrastructure reads as existing conditions ('a facility is confirmed') rather than organizational achievements ('we secured a facility')
The piece avoids 'look what we accomplished' framing in favor of describing what is happening in communities and in people's lives

suggested_rubric.name: Story-First Evidence Integration
suggested_rubric.description: Human narrative always precedes data. Numbers arrive as confirmation of what the reader already feels, not as the opening argument. Scale is expressed by multiplying the named protagonist's outcome outward — the individual becomes the unit of measurement — so the reader is multiplying a known story, not processing an abstraction. Readers should feel a number before they see it.

dimensions.label: A human image or story is established before any impact number is introduced
Data points land as confirmation of the story's significance rather than as a standalone pitch for impact
When numbers reappear in later sections, they are tied back to the narrative context that originally earned them
Scale is expressed by first building a human image of people in the protagonist's position, then letting the count arrive as confirmation of that image — the reader feels the number before seeing it

suggested_rubric.name: Protagonist Continuity
suggested_rubric.description: Named individuals introduced early remain present and active throughout the piece, especially in the closing and at the point of the ask. The protagonist's arc must arrive somewhere — callbacks, echoes, and parallel structure connect opening images to closing images. The final emotional image is the named protagonist in motion, not a generalized outcome or abstracted beneficiary.

dimensions.label: Named individuals from the opening reappear by name in or near the closing section
The protagonist's story has a discernible arc that arrives somewhere rather than being introduced and dropped
Closing images deliberately echo or mirror opening images through parallel actions, callbacks, or repeated motifs
The final emotional image is a specific named person doing something concrete, not a generalized outcome like 'a girl who stays enrolled'
The named protagonist is present and active in the paragraph where the donor ask lands, not just in the setup

suggested_rubric.name: Earned and Specific Ask
suggested_rubric.description: The call to action grows organically from the narrative rather than being appended as a fundraising block. It is specific and bounded — a named dollar amount, a defined number of units, a clear deadline — and connects directly to the protagonist's story so the donor sees the gift as the next chapter of a known outcome, not a separate transaction.

dimensions.label: The ask emerges from the narrative as a logical next step rather than arriving as a tacked-on appeal
The ask includes a specific dollar amount, a defined number of units or people, and/or a clear timeframe
The ask connects directly back to the named protagonist's story, situation, or cohort — the gift is framed in protagonist-sized units
The close creates forward momentum and invitation — the story is unfinished in the reader's mind, resolvable through continued investment — rather than gratitude and closure

suggested_rubric.name: Active, Concrete Language
suggested_rubric.description: Verbs show motion and action rather than states and readiness. At key emotional moments — especially openings and closings — language is concrete and imagistic rather than abstract. The reader should be able to picture someone doing something, not just understand that something is the case.

dimensions.label: Key sentences use verbs of action and motion (walked, walking, negotiated) rather than states (is ready, are able, are at the threshold)
Emotional high points use specific, visual imagery rather than abstract or generalized language
Descriptions reference particular people, places, and moments rather than categories or types
Verb choices are deliberate and precise — each verb earns its place and carries the intended energy (active, volitional, forward)

suggested_rubric.name: Strategic Donor Framing
suggested_rubric.description: Donors are positioned as effective strategic investors whose past decisions produced specific measurable outcomes, not as generous benefactors being thanked. Language borrows from strategy and finance — 'compounds,' 'logical next move,' 'calculus,' 'calculated bet' — to activate the donor's identity as someone who makes smart calls. The ask reads as the next move in an existing strategy, not a new request for help. Crucially, strategic framing must stay anchored to the named protagonist — ROI language or research citations that float free of the individual's story read as program-level abstraction, not as investor-grade evidence.

dimensions.label: Past giving is credited with producing a specific, named outcome rather than appreciated abstractly
Uses strategic or financial vocabulary (e.g., 'compounds,' 'calculus,' 'logical next move,' 'proof,' 'calculated bet') to speak to the donor's investor identity
Donor is made to feel effective and smart rather than generous and appreciated — like someone who ran an experiment that returned results
The ask is framed as a continuation or compounding of an existing strategy, not a new request for help
The piece avoids guilt-based, obligation-based, or desperate framing — the tone is one of strategic respect, not emotional manipulation or pleading
Strategic and ROI language is tied to the named protagonist's specific arc — investor framing that floats free of the individual story reads as abstraction, not evidence

writing_task: Hey! I'm working on a fundraising appeal for a segment of donors we've never really written to directly before — people who gave once, about two years ago, and then lapsed. We have a specific story we want to anchor it around: a woman named Fatima who came to our workforce development program after a factory closure and just got promoted to shift supervisor at her new job eight months later. The challenge is re-engaging people who already said yes once but drifted away, without making them feel guilty for being gone — I want them to feel like they're stepping back into something that's working, not like they're being called out for leaving.

drafts.rubric:
Two years ago, you made a calculated bet. You looked at what workforce development could do for people whose industries had disappeared overnight, and you decided it was worth backing. That judgment was correct.

Fatima knows it was correct. When the factory closed, she walked into a training program and decided she wasn't done yet. Eight months later, she negotiated her way into a shift supervisor role she hadn't held before — not because someone handed it to her, but because she bet on herself and put in the work to make it stick.

She is one of 47 people who moved through the program last year and into stable employment. Forty-seven people who looked at a closed door and found another one.

The next cohort starts in six weeks. Thirty open seats. A gift of $150 funds one person's full training — the same investment that produced Fatima's arc, now compounding into someone else's.

This isn't a new ask. It's the logical next move in a strategy that already has proof. Fatima is managing a shift right now. The question is who else gets that chance.

If you're ready to extend the bet, a gift of $150 before March 15 puts the next person in a seat.
drafts.generic:
A lot has happened since you last heard from us — and we wanted you to be the first to know.

Fatima came to us after the factory where she'd worked for six years suddenly closed. Eight months later, she was promoted to shift supervisor at her new job. That's the kind of thing we get to be part of every day.

We'd love to have you back alongside us. Not because anything was missing when you were gone, but because something good is happening here — and there's still more to do.

Will you rejoin us today?
drafts.preference:
Subject: She's a shift supervisor now — and you were part of that

Two years ago, you made a gift to support our workforce development program. A lot has happened since then — for all of us — and we understand how easily things slip away.

We wanted to share an update, because we think you'll be glad to hear it.

Fatima came to us after the factory where she'd worked for eleven years suddenly closed. She didn't know what came next. Eight months after joining our program, she was promoted to shift supervisor at a new company — a role she applied for with the confidence she built here.

That's what your gift helped make possible.

Things are still moving. People like Fatima are still walking through our doors, and the work you believed in two years ago is still working. We'd love to have you back alongside us.

If you're ready to re-engage, a gift of any size today will go directly toward supporting the next person who needs exactly what Fatima found here. You can give at [link].

Thank you for saying yes once. We hope you'll say it again.

user_reason: Draft A is the clear winner. It frames the donor as a strategic investor ('calculated bet,' 'logical next move'), centers Fatima's agency completely ('she negotiated,' 'she bet on herself'), keeps the non-profit invisible, and ends with a specific ask tied directly to the story — $150, one seat, six weeks, Fatima's arc as proof of concept. That's exactly the structure I want. Draft B is serviceable but has real problems: 'Fatima came to us' and 'the confidence she built here' quietly make us the hero, 'your gift helped make possible' edges toward benefactor framing rather than investor framing, and 'a gift of any size' is the vague ask I'd never approve. The apology-adjacent opener ('we understand how easily things slip away') also risks triggering the guilt dynamic I'm trying to avoid, even if gently. Draft C is the weakest by a significant margin — no specifics, no numbers, Fatima's story is stripped to two sentences with no texture, 'we get to be part of every day' centers the organization, and 'Will you rejoin us today?' is as generic as it gets. It reads like a placeholder, not a finished appeal.

criteria_analysis.name: Community-Centered Voice
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft makes Fatima the grammatical subject throughout — she walked, she decided, she negotiated — and the organization never appears as 'we.' The generic draft uses 'we' repeatedly ('we wanted,' 'we get to be part of') and positions the org as narrator. The original preference draft also uses 'we' and 'our' frequently and includes 'we think you'll be glad to hear it,' centering the organization as intermediary rather than letting Fatima's agency stand alone.

criteria_analysis.name: Strategic Donor Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails this with 'calculated bet,' 'compounding,' 'logical next move,' 'proof,' and 'extend the bet' — all anchored to Fatima's arc and making the donor feel analytically validated. The generic draft has zero strategic language and frames re-engagement as emotional ('something good is happening'). The original preference draft credits the donor's past gift but frames it as generosity ('your gift helped make possible') rather than strategic judgment, and uses no investor-identity vocabulary.

criteria_analysis.name: Earned and Specific Ask
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft delivers a specific ask ($150, 30 seats, March 15 deadline) that grows directly from Fatima's story and the 47-person cohort data — it feels like a logical next chapter. The generic draft has only a vague 'will you rejoin us today?' with no amount, units, or deadline. The original preference draft asks for 'a gift of any size,' which lacks specificity and bounded framing, though it does connect back to Fatima.

criteria_analysis.name: Story-First Evidence Integration
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft establishes Fatima's full arc before introducing '47 people' — the number lands as confirmation of what the reader already feels from her story. The generic draft mentions the promotion but introduces no data at all, so there's nothing to integrate. The original preference draft tells Fatima's story but never introduces any scale data, missing the opportunity to let numbers confirm the narrative.

criteria_analysis.name: Protagonist Continuity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft keeps Fatima present by name through the closing paragraph ('Fatima is managing a shift right now') and places her actively in the ask moment — the final image is her in motion on the shop floor while the reader decides. The generic draft mentions Fatima's promotion early but she vanishes entirely by the close. The original preference draft references 'what Fatima found here' near the ask but the final image is abstract ('the next person who needs') rather than Fatima in concrete motion.

criteria_analysis.name: Active, Concrete Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft uses precise motion verbs: 'walked into,' 'negotiated her way into,' 'looked at a closed door and found another one,' 'managing a shift right now.' The generic draft is competent but relies on state verbs ('was promoted,' 'is happening'). The original preference draft has some active language ('came to us,' 'applied for') but also leans on states ('didn't know what came next,' 'are still walking') and hedged constructions ('we think you'll be glad').

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across every criterion this user cares about — protagonist agency without organizational narration, investor-identity donor framing with signature vocabulary anchored to Fatima's arc, a specific and bounded ask that emerges organically, and Fatima present by name at the close. The original preference draft is a competent lapsed-donor appeal but centers the organization too much and lacks the strategic language and specificity this user demands. The generic draft fails on nearly every dimension the user prioritizes.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is exceptionally clear about what happened, what's needed, and what the reader should do — the specific numbers ($150, 47 people, 30 seats, March 15 deadline) leave no ambiguity. Draft B is clear but vague on the ask and lacks concrete details about what 'rejoin us' means.

Draft A builds a tight logical chain: past investment → proof of results → next opportunity → specific action. Draft B's logic is looser — the transition from Fatima's story to 'we'd love to have you back' feels disconnected, and the reassurance 'nothing was missing when you were gone' introduces an odd, unprompted note.
Both drafts are mechanically clean with no grammatical errors or typos.

Draft A has a purposeful architecture — context, story, data, ask, deadline — each paragraph serving a distinct function. Draft B is brief to the point of feeling underdeveloped, with the ask arriving before sufficient groundwork has been laid.

Draft A's confident, specific prose ('a calculated bet,' 'now compounding into someone else's') is compelling and creates urgency. Draft B's softer, more generic language ('a lot has happened,' 'something good is happening here') lacks the same pull and emotional specificity.

Draft A maintains a consistently direct, respectful, and strategically-minded voice that treats the reader as a smart investor. Draft B's tone is warm and friendly but slightly uncertain — the hedging ('not because anything was missing') undermines its confidence.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is substantially stronger across nearly every dimension: it is more specific, more persuasive, better structured, and more engaging. It treats the reader with intellectual respect, builds a clear argument supported by data, and delivers a precise call to action. Draft B, while pleasant, is too vague and underdeveloped to be comparably effective.

suggestion_text:
- **Community-Centered Voice**: Rubric-guided draft scored 5 and both other drafts scored 3 — this criterion is clearly differentiating and working exactly as intended. Kept unchanged.
- **Strategic Donor Framing**: Rubric-guided draft scored 5 while the original preference draft scored 2 and the generic draft scored 1 — the sharpest gap in the evaluation, confirming this criterion is the rubric's most powerful differentiator. Kept unchanged.
- **Earned and Specific Ask**: Rubric-guided draft scored 5 against scores of 1 and 2 for the other drafts — the criterion is producing the exact specificity ('a gift of any size' vs. '$150, 30 seats, March 15') the user flagged as a non-negotiable. Kept unchanged.
- **Active, Concrete Language**: Rubric-guided draft scored 5 while both other drafts scored 3 — the criterion is differentiating and producing the motion-verb precision the user called out ('she negotiated,' 'she bet on herself'). Kept unchanged.
